title: Conjunction
definition: A conjunction is a word that joins words, phrases, or clauses together.
rules: Coordinating conjunctions join equal parts, remembered by FANBOYS: For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So.
Subordinating conjunctions join a main clause with a dependent clause: because, although, since, if, when, while.
Correlative conjunctions work in pairs: either/or, neither/nor, both/and, not only/but also.
examples: She was tired, but she finished the work.
I stayed home because it was raining.
Although he was late, he didn't apologize.
You can have either tea or coffee.
Not only is she smart, but also very hardworking.

commonMistakes.wrong: Although he was tired, but he finished the race.
commonMistakes.right: Although he was tired, he finished the race.
commonMistakes.why: Don't use both 'although' and 'but' together — choose one.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to go, however I am busy.
commonMistakes.right: I want to go; however, I am busy.
commonMistakes.why: 'However' as a conjunctive adverb needs a semicolon or full stop before it, not a comma joining two full clauses.

quiz.question: Which word completes: 'I was hungry, ___ I ate a sandwich.'
quiz.explanation: 'So' shows the result of being hungry.

quiz.question: Which is a correlative conjunction pair?

quiz.sentence: Although it was raining, but we went out.
quiz.correction: Although it was raining, we went out.
quiz.explanation: Don't use both 'although' and 'but' in the same sentence.
